bio.biography:
Career Highlights
  • Junior World Championships, Planica, 2024 (20km Freestyle - Men, 10km Classic Style - Men)
  • World Youth/Junior Championships, Salt Lake City, 2022 (4 x 7.5km Relay - Men, 7.5km Sprint - Men, 10km Pursuit - Men)
World Championship Experience
  • 2022 IBU Youth World Championships (Soldier Hollow, Utah): 37th in individual / 28th in sprint / 30th in pursuit / 7th in junior men's relay (w/ Maxime Germain, Vincent Bonacci & Bjorn Westervelt)
  • 2021 IBU Youth/Junior World Championships (Obertilliach, Austria) - 59th in youth individual / 40th in youth sprint / 52nd in youth pursuit / 5th in youth relay
More
I began skiing at the age of 2 when we moved to Park City, UT.  I liked downhill skiing but didn't try Nordic until I was 10.  I loved it and started cross country skiing with the Park City Ski Club.  When I was 13, I started biathlon with Soldier Hollow.  When I am not skiing, I love running, hiking, swimming, paddle boarding, traveling, and solving Rubik's cubes.  I also have 4 cats, 2 goats, 2 sheep, 2 peahens, a rabbit, and 12 chickens.

Quick Facts
  • Grayson is the son of Kevin and Lisa Campbell
  • His mother is a teacher and his father is a postal worker
  • Grayson has two siblings, Rhett and Tierney
  • Grayson began diving at age seven and now trains four to five hours a day, six days a week
  • His favorite dive is 5337D on 3-meter and between dives at a meet, Grayson listens to music (he likes anything upbeat)
  • He likes to eat pasta and chicken the night before he competes and oatmeal and eggs the morning of a competition
  • He is majoring in sport management at Texas
  • He enjoys spikeball and watching the NHL, particularly the Washington Capitals

  • Years of participation: 3m Springboard - Men 2024
  • Top finish: 37th – 2024 (3m Springboard - Men)

bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Daughter of James and Cindy McEvoy
  • Has a twin brother Shane
  • Married to Eric Campbell
  • Nickname is AliMac
  • Game Day Mantra: "Take each game, each shot as it comes, and have fun."
  • Best Words of Advice: "Just play, the rest will take care of itself."
Career Highlights
  • Indoor World Cup, Berlin, 2018 (Team - Women)
World Championship Experience
  • Gold - 2021 Indoor Pan American Cup (Spring City, Pa.)
  • Gold - 2017 Indoor Pan American Cup (Georgetown, Guyana)
  • 9th - 2020-21 FIH Pro League (worldwide)
  • 10th - 2018 FIH Hockey Indoor World Cup (Berlin, Germany)
  • Canceled - 2022 FIH Indoor Hockey World Cup (Liège, Belgium)
More
Recovering from one major ACL tear to compete collegiately is a feat in and of itself. McEvoy’s drive and determination have propelled her through two torn ACL’s to play at the collegiate level, win national championships, compete internationally on several Junior National Squads and make the Women’s National Indoor Hockey team roster for 5 years. Even with what she has already accomplished, her dreams have definitely not come to a halt. McEvoy has set her sights on becoming an Olympian in the near future.

Quick Facts
  • Son of Greg and Stephanie Campbell and has two brothers, Caleb and Jacob. His brother, Caleb, played football for the Kansas City Chiefs
  • Born with a rare birth deficiency, fibular hemimelia, Campbell had his right leg amputated at age one.

  • The youngest of three brothers in an athletic family, he gravitated to sports.

  • Played point guard and was the starting quarterback for his high school basketball and football teams.

  • In 2003, Campbell participated in the Endeavor Games, his first exposure to Paralympic track and field.

  • Five years later, he emerged in 2008 as one of the stars of the U.S. Paralympic Track & Field Team, winning his first pair of gold medals in discus and pentathlon at the Paralympic Games Beijing 2008.

  • Today, he is one of the most accomplished Paralympic throwers and made history as the first Paralympian to throw over 60-meters.

  • Hobbies include, reading, watching movies, video games, golf and anything active outdoors.

Paralympic Experience
  • 5-time Paralympian; 5-time Paralympic medalist (5 gold)
    • Paralympic Games Paris 2024, gold (Discus Throw F64 - Men)
    • Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020, gold (Discus Throw F64 - Men)
    • Paralympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, 4th (Discus Throw F44 - Men)
    • Paralympic Games London 2012, gold (Discus Throw F44 - Men)
    • Paralympic Games Beijing 2008, gold (Discus Throw F44 - Men, Pentathlon P44 - Men), 4th (Long Jump T42-44 - Men)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2023 – silver (Discus Throw F64 - Men)
  • Years of participation: Discus Throw F64 - Men 2019, 2023; Discus Throw F44 - Men 2011, 2013, 2015, 2017; 100m T44 - Men 2006; Pentathlon P44 - Men 2006
  • Medals: 7 (4 gold, 3 silver)
    • Gold – 2019 (Discus Throw F64 - Men); 2017 (Discus Throw F44 - Men); 2015 (Discus Throw F44 - Men); 2013 (Discus Throw F44 - Men)
    • Silver – 2023 (Discus Throw F64 - Men); 2011 (Discus Throw F44 - Men); 2006 (Pentathlon P44 - Men)
Other Career Highlights
  • Featured in the 2011 ESPN Magazine Body Issue and won an ESPY award for Best Male Athlete with a Disability in 2013
  • Years of Participation: 2006, 2011, 2013, 2015, 2017, 2019. Medals: 6 (4 gold, 2 silver). Most Recent: 2019 – gold (discus throw).
More

Quick Facts
  • Daughter of Sarah and Geoff Carey. Has a sister, McKay, and two brothers, Max and Will.
  • Born in China with a severe club foot
  • Was adopted and brought back to live in Idaho with her parents and siblings
  • Underwent serial casting and surgery ultimately resulting in correction, however resulting in drop foot along with nerve damage
  • Began pursuing adaptive sports after watching the Olympic Games London 2012 Within
  • Began competing in Paratriathlon, track and field and swimming
  • Her first competition was later that year at the Desert Challenge

Quick Facts

  • Specialty: All-around, vault, uneven bars, balance beam and floor exercise

Biographical Information

Jade Carey began gymnastics at a young age and quickly showed promise in the sport. Her parents owned a gym which allowed her to get involved with gymnastics at just two years old. She attended Mountain Ridge High School and was inducted into the Hall of Fame in January of 2024. She currently attends and competes at Oregon State. 

Olympic Highlights

Jade Carey's Olympic debut at the Tokyo 2020 Olympic Games was marked by both challenges and triumph. After stepping in to replace Simone Biles, she placed eighth in the all-around final and finished eighth in the vault final. Carey concluded her Tokyo 2020 Games experience on a high note, winning gold in the floor exercise final with a score of 14.366, demonstrating her strength in tumbling and precise landings.

At the Paris 2024 Olympic Games, Carey helped power Team USA to gold in the team all-around final and added to her individual medal collection with a bronze in the vault final.

World Championship Highlights

At the 2017 world championships, Jade Carey made an impressive debut on the international stage, earning silver medals in both vault and floor exercise.

The following year at the 2018 world championships, Carey continued to demonstrate her excellence, earning another silver medal, this time on floor exercise.

Carey showcased her value as a team player at the 2022 World Artistic Gymnastics Championships, helping the United States secure the team silver medal.

Early Career

Carey's path to elite gymnastics was somewhat unconventional. Unlike many elite gymnasts who progress through the traditional Junior Olympic program, Carey competed in the Hopes division (a developmental program for young, aspiring elite gymnasts) before making the leap directly to senior elite competition in 2016.

She trained at Arizona Sunrays and her performances in her first senior elite competitions in 2016 set the stage for her rapid rise in the sport.

Carey began her collegiate gymnastics career at Oregon State University while continuing to compete at the elite level. Contributed to the U.S. team's silver medal at the World Championships.

In her NCAA career at Oregon State University, Carey has achieved perfect 10.0 scores on multiple occasions, particularly on floor exercise and vault, demonstrating her exceptional skill level in collegiate competition as well as elite gymnastics.

Olympic Experience
  • 2-time Olympian; 3-time Olympic medalist (2 gold, 1 bronze)
    • Olympic Games Paris 2024, gold (Team - Women), bronze (Vault - Women), 74th (Floor Exercise - Women)
    • Olympic Games Tokyo 2020, gold (Floor Exercise - Women), 8th (Individual All-Around - Women, Vault - Women), 20th (Uneven Bars - Women), 41th (Beam - Women)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2022 – gold (Team - Women, Vault - Women), bronze (Floor Exercise - Women), 6th (Individual All-Around - Women), 11th (Beam - Women), 32th (Uneven Bars - Women)
  • Years of participation: Beam - Women 2022; Floor Exercise - Women 2017, 2019, 2022; Individual All-Around - Women 2022; Team - Women 2019, 2022; Uneven Bars - Women 2022; Vault - Women 2017, 2019, 2022
  • Medals: 7 (3 gold, 3 silver, 1 bronze)
    • Gold – 2022 (Team - Women, Vault - Women); 2019 (Team - Women)
    • Silver – 2019 (Vault - Women); 2017 (Floor Exercise - Women, Vault - Women)
    • Bronze – 2022 (Floor Exercise - Women)
